turns.content: I'm trying to understand the difference between using fuser crate for read-only operations versus implementing a full read-write filesystem with the fuser crate. Can someone clarify this?

The core distinction lies in how you handle the FUSE operation callbacks and whether your implementation requires mutable state management through traits like FuseOperations. For a read-only scenario, you only need to implement the lookup, getattr, readfile, and statfs operations, which keeps your code simpler since you never need to write data back. However, if you want read-write support with the fuser crate, you must also implement setxattr, getxattr, unlink, rmdir, create, mknod, link, rename, mkdir, and truncate, which introduces complexity because those operations require managing file descriptors and handling errors from the kernel.
That makes sense for the callback count. But I'm wondering if there's a way to make read-write mode faster without implementing all these traits, or is it just about how I structure my FuseManager?

Performance doesn't come from skipping traits; it comes from how you manage the Rust runtime overhead and kernel interactions. The fuser crate uses a C library to handle the actual filesystem operations, so your Rust code just needs to correctly map requests to those calls. If you skip implementing write callbacks like truncate or rename, your tool simply cannot perform writes at all, regardless of optimization tricks. The bottleneck isn't the number of traits; it's whether your application logic properly handles the synchronous nature of these kernel calls versus asynchronous ones.
Got it. So if I only care about read-only, maybe I can avoid some of that overhead?

Yes, but the setup cost is already present once you initialize your FUSE instance with the fuser crate. The main advantage in a read-only mode is that you don't need to handle error cases for failed writes or race conditions on file creation. You can focus on optimizing the lookup and readfile paths specifically, which are the most common operations in a read-heavy tool. This means your Rust types like FuseContext will be simpler to reason about, reducing bugs during development.
I'm testing my current implementation with fuser and it seems to work for reading but crashes when I try to write. Is this because I forgot some trait implementations?

It is likely that you are missing specific traits from the FuseOperations enum required for write operations. The fuser crate expects certain methods to be implemented, and if your Rust code omits them, the kernel will reject your request or panic when it tries to execute a write syscall. You need to ensure every operation path through your handler has corresponding trait implementations defined in your struct.
Okay, I'll implement those missing traits. One more thing though—how do I test this without actually mounting it on my system?

You can use unit tests to verify your trait implementations are complete and correct by mocking the FUSE kernel calls in isolation, but for a real integration test you must actually mount your filesystem using the fuser crate's mounting utilities. This ensures your Rust code interacts correctly with the Linux kernel via the C interface provided by the fuser crate.

I've been implementing a custom filesystem using the fuser crate, but I'm struggling to make it behave like a standard Linux mount. It feels similar to how my C library uses libmount and FUSE helpers to create virtual block devices, so I want to ensure my Rust implementation correctly implements the FUSE interface for both read-only and read-write modes.

That's a great parallel; using C libraries with libmount is common when you need low-level control over block devices. The key difference in Rust is that fuser crate handles the FUSE protocol directly, and you don't need to manually manage syscalls like you would with those C helpers. You should think of it as a higher-level abstraction where the crate manages the file operations for you.

I've been trying to implement read-write mode, but I'm getting errors when my code tries to access the FUSE operation parameters. It seems like I need to handle the file open and close lifecycle myself since fuser doesn't give me direct control over that.

You're right; in Rust you do manage the lifecycle because the crate provides traits for file operations rather than wrapping syscalls automatically. When implementing read-write mode, your code needs to explicitly handle opening files and closing them after each operation, which is different from how libmount handles those operations in C.
Okay, so I need to focus on the FUSE operation parameters. Can you explain why this matters for my implementation compared to a simpler read-only approach?

The reason is that in read-write mode your filesystem must validate permissions and handle file locking, whereas read-only mode just needs to verify the data exists. The fuser crate requires you to implement these checks because it doesn't automatically manage permissions like some higher-level abstractions might.

I'm trying to implement a read-only FUSE filesystem for my weekend project using the fuser crate and fuserio, but I keep getting an error saying 'failed to bind socket' when I try to call .bind() on my listener. What went wrong?

It sounds like you're confusing how the FUSE kernel module handles sockets versus your user-space implementation. The fuser crate expects you to create a file descriptor that represents the mount point, not just bind a TCP socket on your own code. Think of it like this: if you try to put a physical bookshelf inside another bookshelf, you'll end up with nothing but empty space because the first shelf is actually part of the system's structure. Similarly, in Rust with FUSE, your filesystem implementation needs to interact directly with the kernel via the fuserio traits, and attempting to bind a regular TCP socket is like trying to use the wrong tool for the job entirely.

Oh, I see. So I shouldn't be using std::net::TcpListener at all? But my code has that everywhere because it worked fine with my previous scripting language where everything was just a simple socket connection...

Exactly, and that's the core gotcha. In your old scripting environment, the runtime handled the low-level details for you, so you only interacted with high-level APIs like .read() or .write(). But in Rust with FUSE, you need to manually manage the lifetime of those file descriptors using traits like BorrowMut and ensure you're calling the correct methods from the fuser crate. The analogy is simpler: think of your scripting language as having a pre-installed kitchen appliance that does everything automatically, whereas Rust requires you to actually build the machine yourself. You must initialize the FUSE kernel module properly before attempting any file operations.

I'm trying to port our existing C filesystem project into Rust using the fuser crate, but I keep hitting performance walls when handling many small files. My current approach assumes that wrapping the C call in a simple Rust function will give me the same low-level efficiency as the original implementation without needing to manage explicit memory allocations or manual synchronization.

That's a common misconception for porting systems code to Rust. The fuser crate actually requires you to implement specific traits like FUSEFilesystem and handle the file_operations callback directly rather than just wrapping existing C logic. You cannot simply call a pre-existing function because the fuser module expects you to provide the actual implementation of read, write, and open operations within your trait bounds.

You're right about needing custom implementations, but I thought Rust's borrow checker would automatically handle all the synchronization concerns for me since it's designed to be safe. If I just implement these traits correctly, shouldn't that eliminate the race conditions entirely without requiring extra manual locking mechanisms?

The borrow checker helps prevent data races at compile time, but FUSE specifically requires you to manage file descriptors and internal state manually because the kernel expects synchronous operations for performance reasons. You cannot offload this responsibility to Rust's ownership system alone; you must explicitly track open files and handle the lifecycle of each connection through your custom struct that implements the required traits.

I see, so I need to implement a wrapper type myself instead of just using standard library types? But if I do that, won't the overhead of Rust's virtual function calls kill any performance gains from porting our C code over?

Yes, you must define your own struct and manually manage the file descriptors. However, Rust is designed to minimize this overhead through zero-cost abstractions. If you implement these traits efficiently without unnecessary allocations or extra indirections, the performance should match the C version because the actual I/O happens in native code while Rust only handles the bookkeeping.

Okay, so I need to create a custom struct that implements FUSEFilesystem and manually manage file descriptors. But wait, if I'm already doing manual management, why do we still need all those specific trait methods? Isn't that redundant compared to just calling the C functions directly like in my original project?

The traits are not redundant; they provide the interface that FUSE needs to communicate with your application. You cannot bypass them because fuser requires these callbacks to be implemented explicitly. Additionally, Rust's standard library types don't support the low-level file descriptor management required here, so you must build a custom wrapper struct that implements the necessary traits and handles the lifecycle of each connection.

I've been reading the docs on how to build a FUSE filesystem using the fuser crate and I still don't get how it connects to my existing Linux setup. It seems like I need to implement some traits but I'm not sure where this fits into what I already know about file systems.

It's totally normal to feel stuck on that first read-through; the docs can be dense. Think of it like this: you're using the fuser crate as a bridge, and your Rust code implements the traits required by FUSE so Linux knows how to handle file reads and writes through your new mount point.

I get that it's a bridge but I'm still confused about which specific functions from the fuser crate I need to call. Does the user need to manually invoke syscalls or does the crate handle that automatically?

The fuser crate handles most of the heavy lifting; your Rust code just needs to implement the Filesystem trait and provide callbacks for operations like open, read, write, and close. The crate itself manages the syscalls so you don't have to manually invoke them.
Okay that helps but I'm still worried about testing this because I haven't done advanced filesystem work before. Can I just compile it and run it?

You can compile it, but you'll need to mount your new FUSE filesystem on a real Linux system using the fuse-utils crate or similar tools to test read-only and read-write operations. Just compiling won't be enough; you need an actual kernel mount point.
